intro:
The Nile (Hebrew Ye'or, "the river"; also Shihor, "the dark/turbid one") was the lifeblood of Egypt and one of the great geographical and theological backdrops of the Exodus. Though the name "Nile" rarely appears in older English Old Testaments — which render it simply "the river" or "the river of Egypt" — no other foreign river so dominates the biblical imagination, for upon its waters Egypt's whole life, wealth, and idolatry depended.

Egypt was famously "the gift of the Nile." Each year the river's flood deposited rich black silt along its banks, turning a ribbon of valley amid the desert into extraordinarily fertile farmland and making Egypt the granary to which the ancient world turned in famine. This dependence shapes the patriarchal narratives: Pharaoh's dream of seven fat and seven thin cows coming up out of the Nile, which Joseph interpreted as years of plenty and famine (Genesis 41:1–4), turns on the river's rise and fall, and it was famine that brought Jacob's family down to Egypt and into the land where they would multiply.

The Nile is woven through the birth and deliverance of Moses. When Pharaoh ordered every Hebrew boy thrown into the Nile (Exodus 1:22), the infant Moses was hidden in a basket among the reeds at the river's edge and drawn out by Pharaoh's daughter — his very name explained as "because I drew him out of the water" (Exodus 2:10). Later the river became the stage of God's judgment on Egypt's gods: the first plague turned its waters to blood so that the fish died and the Egyptians could not drink (Exodus 7:20–21), and from it came the swarms of frogs (Exodus 8:3). The river that Egypt worshiped and that had been ordered to drown Israel's sons was turned by the LORD into an instrument of plague.

The prophets seize on the Nile as the emblem of Egypt's pride and false security. Ezekiel pictures Pharaoh as the great dragon lying in the midst of his streams, boasting, "My Nile is my own; I made it for myself" (Ezekiel 29:3), and announces that God will dry up the river and lay Egypt waste. Jeremiah likens Egypt's surging armies to the rising Nile (Jeremiah 46:7–8), and rebukes Judah for going to drink "the waters of the Nile" — that is, for trusting Egypt rather than the LORD (Jeremiah 2:18). Amos warns that the land will heave and sink "like the Nile of Egypt" under coming judgment (Amos 8:8). Thus the Nile stands in Scripture both as the bounty God provided and as the proud, deceptive river of the world-power whose self-sufficiency the LORD humbles.
